paragraphs.body: Introduction to Fiction Writing
Craft Paper

Similar to other writers, something I feel like I’ve struggled with is dialogue. It’s always seemed like this daunting thing for me to write. Admittedly, it hasn’t even just been the actual creation of dialogue but some of the grammar rules of how to do so. I feel like before this class I’ve been too afraid to admit this and have just made do with what I already knew how to do. I feel like this class allowed me to be less afraid of messing up and to just give it a shot and try to learn. This has also allowed me to spend more time focusing on exactly what I want the characters to say rather than trying to fit it into the formula of grammar I feel comfortable with.

annotations.comment: Glad to hear it! Yes, the grammar holds a lot of people back. Which is understandable, but also too bad!
annotations.context_left: the grammar rules of how to do so. I feel like before this class I’ve been too afraid to admit this
annotations.context_right: have just made do with what I already knew how to do. I feel like this class allowed me to be less

paragraphs.body: In my first bit of writing for this course, I was intimidated to be writing for a room full of people who would critique it. As a result, I avoided dialog as much as I could. Most of the dialog was via text, and when there were characters actually speaking, it was a lecture so there were very little conversations actually taking place. Additionally, something else about my dialog in this first story of mine I didn’t like was it didn’t feel authentic. It was words on a page, but seemed a bit lifeless and weren’t actually in a way that people would speak in real life. Just as Anne Lamott said in Bird by Bird, “Dialogue is the way to nail character, so you have to work on getting the voice right,” (page 64).

annotations.comment: You nicely worked in Bird by Bird!

paragraphs.body: From my Flash Fiction assignment at the beginning of the year, I wrote, “Due to the nature of the course, it can be rather difficult to make up a lab day. Because of this, any missed lab without prior communication will result in a letter grade deduction in your final grade,” (page 2) and I feel like this is something that feels a little lifeless and isn’t true to how people actually speak. In my next story, I think I did better at making the dialogue sound like things people would actually say in real life. That being said, I don’t think my characters Elinor and Madeline have distinct voices. One of Anne Lamott’s suggestions for dialogue in Bird by Bird was “...you should be able to identify each character by what he or she says,” (page 63). I think this is something that my story currently lacks.

annotations.comment: I agree. But this is just a draft! You would have time to refine their voices.

paragraphs.body: “Telling everyone about my life as if I’m just some character in a story-”
”You are just a character in a story, Maddie. You aren’t real-”

I feel like something I can do for revision in this story is really strengthen the divide between how they both talk. I think it would add a lot to my story having Madeline speak very thoughtfully and have Elinor just blurt things out.

annotations.comment: Great idea. The transition between these sections feels a little clunky.

paragraphs.body: A story from The Best American Short Stories that I felt really inspired me in writing dialogue was Halloween. I felt like this story's dialogue felt like you were actually there listening to it.

annotations.comment: Ah, that’s such a good one.

paragraphs.body: “Okay.”
“And remember. Easy breezy. Lemon squeezy.”
“What?”
“You don’t know that one? Customer Service 101. If you feel yourself getting moody or sentimental, you just chant that in your head, and it’ll get you back on track.”

I feel like this dialogue really stood out to me in how simple yet effective it was. I knew exactly who was talking, and it seemed authentic. What I really liked though was how it fit into the rest of the story. Instead of having the dialogue written in a similar format and similar lengths throughout the story, the dialogue in Halloween really played around with these things which inspires me to really make dialogue work for my story rather than getting my story to work for my dialogue.

annotations.comment: You don’t need this. Just “This dialogue really stood out to me” works.

annotations.context_right: really stood out to me in how simple yet effective it was. I knew exactly who was talking, and it s

paragraphs.body: Something else I feel like really helped my dialogue was our class exercise of writing the same scene in different ways. I feel like this really showed me how essentially powerful dialogue is to a story and how it can tell more about a character than over describing them yourself. I feel like this exercise also showed me how important it is to support your dialogue.

annotations.comment: Notice how you fell into a pattern here of “I feel like…”?
annotations.context_right: really helped my dialogue was our class exercise of writing the same scene in different ways. I feel

paragraphs.body: “The tree is going to fall,” she said.
“Where will it fall?” he asked.
“Where we are standing,” she said. (Taylor, April 1)
This was my “boring” version of the exercise. It then became:

The cool autumn breeze swept across the farm, sending a swirl of dust flying in every direction before it collided with the old oak tree that stood in the center of the open land. Two siblings stood beside it, staring up at the tree as it swayed violently in the autumn air.
“The tree is going to fall,” Susan attempted to yell over the sound of the wind.
“Where will it fall?” Her brother Joshua asked as he stared around the farm land that surrounded them.
“Where we’re standing, we need to move!” Susan gasped as a thick branch flew off of the oak towards them. (Taylor, April 1)

I feel like this exercise, but also other things we’ve learned in this class allowed for me to expand my use of dialogue and it has become something I can use to help my story rather than something for me to fear. Instead of remaining so stuck in the formula I’ve always used, I was able to break out of it.  ‘“Charlotte—” The young woman started, cheeks almost as red as her hair. “Um…but everyone just calls me Charlie,”’ (Taylor, 1). I found this allowed me to make my characters come more to life and for their dialogue to seem more authentic.

annotations.comment: A nice paper, Adrian, thank you. A couple of glitches and it came in late so a B+ feels right. Thanks for a great semester!
